C:
Use managed Postgres. For a 5-person team, that's almost always the right call, and the burden of proof should be on self-hosting.

The core reason is that a 5-person team has no spare operational capacity. Managed Postgres (RDS/Aurora, Cloud SQL, Supabase, Neon, Crunchy Bridge, etc.) absorbs the work that otherwise silently lands on whoever is least busy: automated backups with point-in-time recovery, minor-version patching, failover, replication, monitoring, and disk/connection scaling. Self-hosting means someone on your team owns all of that 24/7 — including the 3 a.m. page when the disk fills or replication breaks. At five people, that person is also your lead engineer, and an hour they spend on `pg_basebackup` tuning is an hour not spent on the product. The managed premium (often 20–40% over raw compute) is far cheaper than that engineer's time and the cost of a botched recovery.

Self-hosting only wins under fairly specific conditions, and you should pick it only if one of these genuinely applies:
- **Cost at scale** — if the database is large and steady-state (think tens of TB or very high, predictable throughput), the managed markup can become real money. That's not where a 5-person team usually is.
- **A hard requirement managed can't meet** — a specific extension or Postgres patch the provider doesn't allow, an air-gapped/on-prem compliance mandate, or data-residency rules with no managed option in-region.
- **You already have deep ops muscle** — an SRE who runs Postgres in their sleep and wants it. Even then, weigh it against opportunity cost.

If none of those is true, managed is the default.

A couple of practical notes for choosing among managed options. If you want the least operational thought and usage-based/scale-to-zero pricing, Neon or Supabase suit small teams well; Supabase also bundles auth and APIs if that's useful. If you're already on AWS/GCP and want the most boring, battle-tested path, RDS for Postgres (not necessarily Aurora unless you need its scaling) is the safe pick. Whatever you choose, confirm two things before committing: that point-in-time recovery is enabled and that you've actually tested a restore — managed backups are worthless if no one has verified they come back.

One hedge worth knowing: managed Postgres is standard Postgres, so the lock-in is mostly operational, not structural. If you outgrow the economics later, migrating to self-hosted is a `pg_dump`/logical-replication exercise, not a rewrite. That asymmetry is another reason to start managed — it's the easily reversible choice.

P:
**Recommendation: use managed Postgres.** For a 5-person team it's almost always the right call, and the burden of proof belongs to anyone arguing for self-hosting.

The reasoning in one line: a five-person team has no spare operational capacity, and managed Postgres buys that capacity back for far less than it costs in engineer time.

What you're actually paying for is the work that otherwise lands silently on whoever is least busy — which, at this size, is usually your lead engineer. Managed providers (RDS, Aurora, Cloud SQL, Supabase, Neon, Crunchy Bridge) handle automated backups with point-in-time recovery, minor-version patching, failover, replication, monitoring, and disk and connection scaling. Self-hosting means a person on your team owns all of that, including the 3 a.m. page when the disk fills or replication breaks. Every hour spent tuning `pg_basebackup` is an hour not spent on the product. The infrastructure premium for managed is real but modest, and it's trivial next to the fully-loaded cost of those engineer-hours — plus the tail risk of a recovery that doesn't work the one time you need it.

Choose self-hosting only if at least one of these is concretely true today, not "might be someday":

- **Cost at real scale.** If the database is large and steady-state — think tens of terabytes or very high, predictable throughput — the managed markup turns into actual money. A 5-person team is rarely there yet.
- **A hard requirement managed can't meet.** A specific extension or Postgres patch your provider won't allow, an air-gapped or on-prem compliance mandate, or data-residency rules with no managed option in your region.
- **You already have deep ops muscle that wants the job.** An SRE who runs Postgres in their sleep and would rather own it. Even then, weigh it against what else that person could be building.

If none of those holds, managed is the default — don't overthink it.

For choosing among managed options, two decisive defaults. If you're already on AWS or GCP, pick RDS for Postgres (or Cloud SQL) — the boring, battle-tested path; reach for Aurora only if you specifically need its read-scaling or storage model, not by reflex. If you're greenfield and want the least operational thought plus scale-to-zero pricing, Neon or Supabase suit small teams well, and Supabase also bundles auth and APIs if that's useful to you.

Whatever you pick, confirm two things before you commit: that point-in-time recovery is enabled, and that you've actually run a test restore. Untested backups are the most common way a managed database still loses data — the backup that was never verified is the one that fails.

One hedge worth knowing: managed Postgres is standard Postgres, so the lock-in is operational, not structural. If you outgrow the economics later, moving to self-hosted is a `pg_dump` or logical-replication exercise, not a rewrite. That asymmetry is the clincher — start managed because it's the easily reversible choice, and let scale, not speculation, be what eventually pushes you off it.
